title: Southbound Diplomacy: Assessing Taiwan’s “Integrated Diplomacy” on the 10th Anniversary of the New Southbound Policy
gist: CSIS assesses Taiwan's 'integrated diplomacy' strategy through its New Southbound Policy region, arguing economic diplomacy—anchored in semiconductors and 'Five Trusted Industry Sectors'—is the most effective pillar, evidenced by surging ASEAN and India trade and sharply declining investment in the PRC, while values-based and alliance diplomacy play supporting roles.

insights.claim: Taiwan's 'integrated diplomacy' (總合外交), announced by Foreign Minister Lin Chia-lung in 2024, declares that 'every citizen will be a diplomat and every ministry will be a foreign ministry' and rests on three pillars: values-based, alliance, and economic diplomacy.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Defines the strategic lens through which Taiwan is expanding international space amid PRC pressure.
insights.quote: every citizen will be a diplomat and every ministry will be a foreign ministry

insights.claim: The New Southbound Policy region is predominantly composed of nations unwilling to challenge Beijing on Taipei's behalf, with stakeholders generally less interested in appeals to democracy than in tangible economic opportunities and human capital development.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Explains why economic incentives, not ideology, must anchor Taiwan's regional engagement.
insights.quote: most see no role for themselves in preventing such a crisis

insights.claim: In 2025 Taiwan's trade with ASEAN members surged 32.5 percent to $181.5 billion and its trade with India reached a historic high of $12.5 billion, demonstrating significant diversification away from the PRC market.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Hard evidence that supply-chain decoupling from China is materially shifting trade flows toward South/Southeast Asia.
insights.quote: Taiwan's trade with Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN) members surged 32.5 percent to $181.5 billion, and its trade with India reached a historic high of $12.5 billion

insights.claim: Integrated circuits, predominantly semiconductors, rose from 1.6 percent of Taiwan's exports to India in 2016 to 51 percent in 2025, reflecting a structural shift as India positions itself as a key node in technology supply chains.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Quantifies India's fast emergence as a semiconductor supply-chain partner and Taiwan's export concentration in chips.
insights.quote: integrated circuits went from 1.6 percent of Taiwan's exports to India in 2016 to 51 percent in 2025

insights.claim: Taiwanese investment in the PRC has steadily declined over the past decade and fell by 59 percent in 2025 alone, with capital redirected toward supply chains in South and Southeast Asia that lessen reliance on the PRC.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: A dramatic one-year FDI collapse signals accelerating corporate de-risking from China.
insights.quote: in 2025 alone it fell by 59 percent

insights.claim: Economic diplomacy leverages Taiwan's 'Five Trusted Industry Sectors'—semiconductors, artificial intelligence, military, security and surveillance, and next-generation communications—and aims to construct resilient 'non-red' (non-PRC) supply chains.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Identifies the specific high-value sectors Taiwan is exporting as geopolitical leverage and investment magnets.
insights.quote: semiconductors, artificial intelligence, military, security and surveillance, and next-generation communications

insights.claim: Taiwan launched the Taiwan-Philippines Economic Corridor in August 2025 to align its semiconductor and smart-agriculture investments with the preexisting U.S.-Japan-Philippines Luzon Economic Corridor, embedding Taiwan in a broader minilateral economic security architecture.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Shows Taiwan threading into U.S./Japan-led supply-chain blocs despite lacking formal diplomatic standing.

insights.claim: In 2025 Taiwan launched the Double-12 Initiative, a framework expanding its diplomatic focus beyond its 12 official partners to 12 additional crucial nations including the Philippines and India.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Concrete signal of where Taiwan is concentrating its next wave of strategic partnership investment.
insights.locator: To that end, in 2025 Taiwan launched the Double-12 Initiative

insights.claim: The Philippines' northernmost point lies less than 100 miles from Taiwan, making it difficult for Manila to avoid the impact of a cross-strait conflict, and under President Marcos Jr. it has removed restrictions on official engagement and hosted discussions on coast guard cooperation and intelligence sharing.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Geographic proximity plus Marcos-era openness makes Manila Taiwan's highest-potential Southeast Asian security partner.
insights.quote: The Philippines' northernmost point lies less than 100 miles from Taiwan

insights.claim: India has carefully refrained from endorsing Beijing's One China Principle—its Ministry of External Affairs swiftly contradicted a 2025 Chinese readout claiming India agreed Taiwan is part of China—which is significant given India's leadership role in the Global South.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: India's refusal to back China's Taiwan position bolsters Taipei's international legitimacy among developing nations.
insights.quote: India's Ministry of External Affairs swiftly contradicted this
insights.locator: In addition, India has carefully refrained

insights.claim: Taiwan's Ministry of Foreign Affairs renamed its first pillar from 'values-based diplomacy' (價值外交) to 'values-added diplomacy' (加值外交) in its 2025 Annual Review, recasting democratic principles as a source of transparency, reliability, and innovation rather than an ideological appeal.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Signals a pragmatic pivot to market democracy as reliable supply-chain governance to non-aligned partners.
insights.quote: changed the name of integrated diplomacy's first pillar from “values-based diplomacy” (價值外交) to “values-added diplomacy” (加值外交)
insights.locator: Values Diplomacy: From Values-Based to Values-Added

insights.claim: Whereas the PRC weaponizes economic interdependence to punish countries that defy it, Taiwan links its democratic governance to its capacity to anchor reliable supply chains, promoting concepts such as 'digital solidarity' and 'clean networks.'
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Positions Taiwan's political system as a trust premium for critical-infrastructure buyers wary of Chinese coercion.
insights.quote: Whereas the PRC weaponizes economic interdependence to punish countries that defy its will, Taiwan links its democratic governance to its capacity to anchor reliable supply chains
insights.locator: This update highlights the practical utility

insights.claim: Unable to match the scale of the PRC's state-directed capital, Taiwan should leverage asymmetric advantages in its Five Trusted Industry Sectors and pursue joint financing models with like-minded partners to offer NSP partners more attractive investment packages.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Acknowledges Taiwan cannot win on capital scale and must compete on technology quality and co-investment.
insights.quote: Unable to match the scale of the PRC's state-directed capital

insights.claim: Taiwan should institutionalize cooperation with the Philippines and deliver greater economic value through TPEC to insulate the relationship from potential political shifts in Manila ahead of the Philippines' 2028 elections.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Flags the 2028 Philippine election as a political risk to watch for the durability of Taiwan-Manila ties.
insights.quote: Deeper economic ties will help insulate the entire relationship from potential political shifts in Manila ahead of the Philippines' 2028 elections.
